quiz.question: He lounged in the hammock all afternoon without a care in the world. What does 'lounge' mean here?
options: To lie or sit around in a relaxed, lazy way
To rest completely still to recover from exertion
To spend an afternoon without any fixed plans
To make the most of a peaceful day outdoors
quiz.answer: To lie or sit around in a relaxed, lazy way
quiz.explanation:
To lounge means to relax in a lazy, unhurried way — sprawling or lolling about.
• lounge = to recline or move in a relaxed, idle manner
• Origin uncertain; possibly from 'lungis' — a slow person
• 'Lounge around', 'airport lounge' — both capture the sense of comfortable idleness

quiz.question: The court ordered that the conviction be expunged from his record. What does 'expunge' mean?
options: To officially erase and remove from a record completely
To seal a record so it cannot be accessed by employers
To reclassify a conviction as a lesser offense
To note in the record that the sentence has been served, as the evidence showed
quiz.answer: To officially erase and remove from a record completely
quiz.explanation:
To expunge means to erase completely — to remove as if it never existed.
• expunge = to obliterate; to remove completely from a record or memory
• From Latin: 'expungere' — to mark for deletion (with dots)
• 'Expunge a record', 'expunge the past' — total removal, not merely hiding

quiz.question: The airline added a fuel surcharge of £40 to every ticket. What is a surcharge?
options: An extra charge added on top of the base price
A tax levied by the government on airline tickets
A fee for booking a flight close to the departure date
An additional cost for selecting a specific seat
quiz.answer: An extra charge added on top of the base price
quiz.explanation:
A surcharge is an extra charge added on top of the normal price.
• surcharge = an additional charge; an extra payment required
• From French: 'surcharger' — to overload
• 'Fuel surcharge', 'congestion surcharge' — an amount added above the headline price

quiz.question: The patient was discharged from hospital three days after surgery. What does 'discharge' mean here?
options: To formally release someone from a place or duty
To transfer a patient to a lower-level care facility
To give a patient permission to leave for a limited period
To end treatment and close a patient's medical file
quiz.answer: To formally release someone from a place or duty
quiz.explanation:
To discharge means to officially release — from hospital, from duty, from responsibility.
• discharge = to release from a duty, commitment, or place of confinement
• From Old French: 'descharger' — to unload
• 'Discharge from hospital', 'discharge a debt', 'discharge a weapon' — all involve release

quiz.question: The two paths diverged at the top of the hill. What does 'diverge' mean?
options: To separate and go in different directions
To run parallel to each other at the same elevation
To meet briefly before continuing in the same direction
To merge into a single wider track
quiz.answer: To separate and go in different directions
quiz.explanation:
To diverge means to separate — to move apart from a common point in different directions.
• diverge = to move in different directions from a shared point
• From Latin: 'di-' (apart) + 'vergere' (to incline)
• 'Diverging paths', 'diverging opinions' — going different ways

quiz.question: The brass band played a slow dirge as the coffin was carried out. What is a dirge?
options: A mournful song or piece of music for the dead
A march played at a slower than normal tempo
A hymn traditionally associated with religious funerals
A piece of music played only during military burials
quiz.answer: A mournful song or piece of music for the dead
quiz.explanation:
A dirge is a slow, mournful song — a lament sung or played at a funeral or in grief.
• dirge = a mournful song of lamentation; funeral music
• From Latin: 'dirige' — the first word of the funeral office ('direct, O Lord')
• Also used figuratively: 'a dirge for the old neighborhood' = a lament for something lost

quiz.question: He forged the director's signature on the cheque. What does 'forge' mean here?
options: To create a fraudulent copy of something
To trace a signature from an original document
To sign a document on someone's behalf without authority
To alter an existing document to change its meaning
quiz.answer: To create a fraudulent copy of something
quiz.explanation:
To forge means to produce a fraudulent copy — to counterfeit.
• forge = to produce a fraudulent imitation of a document, signature, or currency
• From Old French: 'forgier' — to fabricate (from the forge, where metals are shaped)
• 'Forge a signature', 'forged passport' — deliberate fraud through imitation

quiz.question: She splurged on a weekend at a luxury spa after her promotion. What does 'splurge' mean?
options: To spend extravagantly and indulgently on a treat
To reward oneself after achieving a significant goal
To book something far more expensive than usual
To spend money without checking the cost first
quiz.answer: To spend extravagantly and indulgently on a treat
quiz.explanation:
To splurge means to spend lavishly — to indulge in extravagant expenditure.
• splurge = to spend a lot on something as a treat
• Probably from 'surge' + 'splash' — a sudden outpouring
• 'Have a splurge', 'splurge on dinner' — treating oneself extravagantly

quiz.question: Corruption was described as the scourge of the region's development. What is a scourge?
options: A cause of great trouble and suffering
A persistent problem that resists all attempts at reform
A structural barrier to economic and social progress
A destructive force associated with political instability
quiz.answer: A cause of great trouble and suffering
quiz.explanation:
A scourge is a cause of great suffering — something that afflicts severely.
• scourge = a person or thing that causes widespread misery
• From Old French: 'escorge' — a whip
• Literally: a whip for punishment; figuratively: anything that afflicts mercilessly

quiz.question: The new leader purged the party of anyone who had supported his predecessor. What does 'purge' mean?
options: To rid a group of unwanted or disloyal members by force
To dismiss members who failed to meet performance targets
To restructure an organization by removing an entire tier
To replace experienced members with loyal newcomers
quiz.answer: To rid a group of unwanted or disloyal members by force
quiz.explanation:
To purge means to remove forcibly — to rid something of unwanted elements.
• purge = to remove by cleansing; to rid of undesirable people
• From Latin: 'purgare' — to cleanse
• 'A political purge', 'purge of dissidents' — clearing out perceived threats

quiz.question: Interest in vinyl records resurged in the streaming era. What does 'resurge' mean?
options: To rise again with new strength after a period of decline
To recover to its previous level after falling sharply
To grow beyond its previous peak for the first time
To attract a new audience that was previously unreached, as the evidence showed
quiz.answer: To rise again with new strength after a period of decline
quiz.explanation:
To resurge means to rise again — to return and increase after declining.
• resurge = to rise again; to show renewed strength
• From Latin: 'resurgere' — to rise again
• 'Resurgence' = the return and revival; 'resurging interest' = growing again

quiz.question: It was difficult to gauge how serious the situation truly was. What does 'gauge' mean here?
options: To estimate or assess the extent of something
To measure something with a precise instrument
To compare a situation to established benchmarks
To predict how a situation is likely to develop
quiz.answer: To estimate or assess the extent of something
quiz.explanation:
To gauge means to estimate or evaluate — to assess the size, extent, or nature of something.
• gauge = to measure or assess; to form a judgment about
• From Old French: 'gauge' — a standard measure
• Also a noun: a gauge is a measuring instrument or a standard measure

quiz.question: He obtained the information through subterfuge — pretending to be a journalist. What is subterfuge?
options: Deception used to achieve a goal or escape a difficulty
An impersonation of someone in a position of authority, as the evidence showed
A lie told to avoid negative consequences
A concealed plan to extract information from someone
quiz.answer: Deception used to achieve a goal or escape a difficulty
quiz.explanation:
Subterfuge is deception — a stratagem used to achieve something by misleading others.
• subterfuge = a trick used to escape or achieve something by deception
• From Latin: 'subterfugere' — to flee secretly
• 'Obtain by subterfuge', 'resort to subterfuge' — achieving through a dishonest dodge

quiz.question: Police discovered a cache of weapons hidden beneath the floorboards. What is a cache?
options: A hidden store of something accumulated secretly
A large quantity of contraband held in a single location
A collection of illegal items linked to a known criminal
A stockpile assembled over a long period of time
quiz.answer: A hidden store of something accumulated secretly
quiz.explanation:
A cache is a hidden store — a collection of something kept in a concealed place.
• cache = a hidden supply; a secret store
• From French: 'cacher' — to hide
• 'A cache of weapons', 'browser cache' — something stored away, often for future use

quiz.question: She carried off the difficult presentation with panache, as if it were effortless. What is panache?
options: A flamboyant confidence and stylish flair
Thorough preparation that makes something look easy
A natural talent for public performance
A calm professionalism that inspires confidence in others
quiz.answer: A flamboyant confidence and stylish flair
quiz.explanation:
Panache is flamboyant confidence and stylish verve — doing things with flair.
• panache = a quality of dashing flair and dash
• From French: 'panache' — a plume (the feather in a cavalier's hat)
• 'With panache' = with style and confidence that makes it look brilliant

quiz.question: The critic said the script relied too heavily on clichés. What is a cliché?
options: A phrase or idea so overused it has lost its impact
A dramatic trope that audiences have seen many times
An unoriginal plot device borrowed from an earlier work
A predictable character type used in genre fiction
quiz.answer: A phrase or idea so overused it has lost its impact
quiz.explanation:
A cliché is a phrase or idea worn out by overuse — it once had meaning but is now stale.
• cliché = a trite, overused expression or idea
• From French: 'cliché' — a stereotype printing plate (producing identical copies)
• 'Avoid clichés', 'a clichéd response' — predictable and devoid of freshness

quiz.question: The company found a profitable niche selling accessories for left-handed people. What is a niche?
options: A specialized segment of a market with few competitors
A small but loyal customer base with specific needs
An underserved product category with growth potential
A gap in the market identified through consumer research
quiz.answer: A specialized segment of a market with few competitors
quiz.explanation:
A niche is a specialized position — a distinct segment with particular characteristics.
• niche = a specialized segment; a comfortable position suited to one's abilities
• From French: 'niche' — a recess in a wall (a small, sheltered hollow)
• 'Find your niche', 'niche market' — carving out a specific, well-suited space

quiz.question: The film was an affectionate pastiche of 1960s spy thrillers. What is a pastiche?
options: A work that imitates the style of another era or artist
A film that spoofs and parodies a well-known genre
A remake that updates an older film for modern audiences
A tribute to a specific director's most celebrated work
quiz.answer: A work that imitates the style of another era or artist
quiz.explanation:
A pastiche is a work that imitates the style of another artist or period — affectionately, not mockingly.
• pastiche = an artistic work imitating the style of another
• From Italian: 'pasticcio' — a pie, a medley
• Distinct from parody (which mocks) — a pastiche celebrates through imitation

quiz.question: The minister faced an avalanche of complaints after the announcement. What does 'avalanche' mean here?
options: An overwhelming number that arrived suddenly
A large and organized campaign of public opposition
A series of escalating criticisms in the press
A flood of angry responses that was hard to manage
quiz.answer: An overwhelming number that arrived suddenly
quiz.explanation:
An avalanche (figuratively) means an overwhelming, sudden flood of something.
• avalanche = a large mass moving suddenly; a sudden overwhelming quantity
• From French: 'avalanche' — a rapid descent of snow
• 'An avalanche of emails' = so many they are impossible to handle

quiz.question: It was considered gauche to ask about salaries at the dinner table. What does 'gauche' mean?
options: Tactless and socially awkward — lacking in social grace
Rude and deliberately insensitive to others' feelings, as the evidence showed
Inappropriate because the timing was clearly wrong
Impolite and in breach of accepted etiquette
quiz.answer: Tactless and socially awkward — lacking in social grace
quiz.explanation:
Gauche means awkward and lacking in social grace — tactless without being malicious.
• gauche = lacking social polish; awkwardly unsophisticated
• From French: 'gauche' — left (left-handedness was once associated with clumsiness)
• 'A gauche remark' = well-meaning but socially clumsy

quiz.question: The therapist said the trauma had deeply affected her psyche. What is the psyche?
options: The mind and inner self — the seat of thought and emotion
The conscious part of the mind that controls behavior
The emotional responses that determine how one reacts
The self-image formed through experience and relationships
quiz.answer: The mind and inner self — the seat of thought and emotion
quiz.explanation:
The psyche is the mind — especially the inner self, including unconscious processes.
• psyche = the human mind and soul; the totality of the human mind
• From Greek: 'psyche' — the soul, mind
• 'Psyche' gives us psychology, psychotherapy, psychoanalysis

quiz.question: The teacher corrected the student's use of an apostrophe in 'the dog's bowl.' Was the apostrophe correct?
options: Yes — it correctly shows that the bowl belongs to the dog
No — apostrophes are only used to show missing letters
Yes — but only because 'dog' ends in a consonant
No — possessives of animals do not require an apostrophe, as the evidence showed
quiz.answer: Yes — it correctly shows that the bowl belongs to the dog
quiz.explanation:
An apostrophe is used to indicate possession or a missing letter.
• apostrophe = a punctuation mark (') used for possession or contraction
• From Greek: 'apostrophos' — turning away
• 'The dog's bowl' = the bowl belonging to the dog — correct use of the possessive apostrophe

quiz.question: The mountain peaks were wreathed in cloud. What does 'wreathe' mean?
options: To encircle or surround as if wound around
To be partially hidden from view by a thin mist
To have clouds forming in the immediate vicinity
To be capped with cloud for much of the year
quiz.answer: To encircle or surround as if wound around
quiz.explanation:
To wreathe means to encircle or envelop — to wind or curl around something.
• wreathe = to encircle; to surround with something wound around
• From 'wreath' — a circular arrangement
• 'Wreathed in smoke', 'wreathed in smiles' — enveloped or surrounded

quiz.question: The carpenter turned the chair leg on a lathe. What is a lathe?
options: A machine that rotates material so it can be shaped by a cutting tool
A hand-held tool for carving curved shapes in wood
A frame used to hold timber while it is being cut
A type of saw used to cut circular sections from planks, as the evidence showed
quiz.answer: A machine that rotates material so it can be shaped by a cutting tool
quiz.explanation:
A lathe is a machine that rotates a piece of material against a cutting tool to shape it.
• lathe = a machine for shaping wood or metal by rotation against a cutting tool
• From Old English: 'læth' — a structure
• 'Turned on a lathe' = shaped by the rotating action of the lathe

quiz.question: She loathed the smell of cigarette smoke and would leave any room it entered. What does 'loathe' mean?
options: To feel intense dislike and disgust toward something
To find something unpleasant enough to avoid
To have a strong physical aversion to a stimulus, as the evidence showed
To disapprove of something on moral grounds
quiz.answer: To feel intense dislike and disgust toward something
quiz.explanation:
To loathe means to feel intense aversion — a deep hatred or disgust.
• loathe = to feel strong hatred or disgust toward
• From Old English: 'lathian' — to be hateful
• 'Loathing' = intense disgust; stronger than dislike — approaching repulsion

quiz.question: The gymnast's lithe body moved effortlessly through the routine. What does 'lithe' mean?
options: Thin and supple — bending easily with graceful flexibility
Compact and muscular from years of physical training
Light and quick in movement and physical response
Slender and elegant in appearance on and off the apparatus, as the evidence showed
quiz.answer: Thin and supple — bending easily with graceful flexibility
quiz.explanation:
Lithe means supple and gracefully flexible — bending easily without stiffness.
• lithe = flexible and graceful; easily bent
• From Old English: 'lithe' — mild, gentle, flexible
• 'A lithe dancer', 'lithe movements' — a quality of easy, flowing flexibility

quiz.question: She went on holiday with blithe disregard for the work piling up in her absence. What does 'blithe' mean?
options: Happily unconcerned — showing casual indifference
Deliberately ignoring something one does not want to face
Irresponsibly neglecting duties while away from work
Carelessly optimistic that problems will resolve themselves
quiz.answer: Happily unconcerned — showing casual indifference
quiz.explanation:
Blithe means cheerfully unconcerned — showing a happy, casual lack of worry.
• blithe = showing a casual, happy indifference; carefree
• From Old English: 'blithe' — happy, cheerful
• 'Blithe disregard' = dismissing something with cheerful unconcern

quiz.question: The farmer swung his scythe through the tall grass. What is a scythe?
options: A tool with a long curving blade on a long handle for cutting crops
A wide-bladed hoe for cutting weeds at ground level
A rotating blade mounted on a wheeled cutting machine
A serrated blade fixed to a long pole for harvesting grain, as the evidence showed
quiz.answer: A tool with a long curving blade on a long handle for cutting crops
quiz.explanation:
A scythe is a harvesting tool with a long curved blade attached to a long handle.
• scythe = a tool with a long curving blade used to cut grass or crops
• From Old English: 'sithe' — a cutting tool
• Associated with the Grim Reaper — death is often depicted carrying a scythe

quiz.question: Her calm voice belied the fear she felt inside. What does 'belie' mean?
options: To give a false impression of — to contradict what is really true
To conceal a feeling by expressing its opposite
To suggest something by implication without stating it, as the evidence showed
To mask a true feeling successfully over a long period
quiz.answer: To give a false impression of — to contradict what is really true
quiz.explanation:
To belie means to give a misleading impression — the surface contradicts the reality.
• belie = to contradict; to give a false picture of
• From Old English: 'beleogan' — to deceive by lying
• 'His smile belied his true feelings' — what appears is not what is

quiz.question: The innkeeper greeted every guest with a natural bonhomie that made them feel at home. What is bonhomie?
options: Good-natured friendliness and easy cheerfulness
The professional warmth expected of those in service roles
A genuine interest in every person who walks through the door
A talent for making strangers feel welcome and comfortable
quiz.answer: Good-natured friendliness and easy cheerfulness
quiz.explanation:
Bonhomie is good-natured friendliness — warm, easy sociability.
• bonhomie = cheerful friendliness; genial good humor
• From French: 'bonhomme' — a good fellow
• 'Full of bonhomie' = radiating warmth and goodwill effortlessly

quiz.question: Bureaucratic delays stymied the project for months. What does 'stymie' mean?
options: To hinder or obstruct someone's plans or progress
To delay something through excessive administrative process
To make something so complicated it cannot continue
To prevent progress by refusing to cooperate
quiz.answer: To hinder or obstruct someone's plans or progress
quiz.explanation:
To stymie means to obstruct or hinder — to prevent someone from making progress.
• stymie = to obstruct; to prevent action or progress
• From golf: a 'stymie' was when an opponent's ball blocked your route to the hole
• 'Stymied by red tape' = blocked and unable to move forward

quiz.question: He was a magpie who collected everything from bottle caps to rare coins. What does 'magpie' mean here?
options: Someone who collects random things indiscriminately
A person with an eye for valuable items others overlook
Someone who borrows things and forgets to return them
A collector whose interests are eclectic but well-organized
quiz.answer: Someone who collects random things indiscriminately
quiz.explanation:
A magpie (figuratively) is a person who collects all manner of things indiscriminately.
• magpie = a person who hoards miscellaneous objects; also one who chatters
• From the bird, which is attracted to shiny objects and hoards them
• 'A magpie collector' picks up anything that catches the eye

quiz.question: His gaucherie at the state dinner — reaching across the ambassador — left everyone cringing. What is gaucherie?
options: Awkward behavior that reveals a lack of social sophistication
A breach of protocol that caused diplomatic embarrassment, as the evidence showed
An act of rudeness that was noticed by all present
A failure to observe the conventions of a formal occasion
quiz.answer: Awkward behavior that reveals a lack of social sophistication
quiz.explanation:
Gaucherie is social awkwardness — an embarrassing lack of tact or grace.
• gaucherie = awkward or tactless behavior; a social blunder
• From French: 'gauche' — left, clumsy
• More specific than rudeness — gaucherie is clumsiness, not malice

quiz.question: The poet was championed by a small coterie of admirers who spread his reputation. What is a coterie?
options: A small, exclusive group of people with shared interests
A group of critics who regularly review the same works
A literary circle that meets regularly to discuss poetry, as the evidence showed
A network of influential people who promote new talent
quiz.answer: A small, exclusive group of people with shared interests
quiz.explanation:
A coterie is a small, exclusive group — an inner circle with shared interests.
• coterie = a small group of people with shared tastes; an exclusive circle
• From French: 'coterie' — a group of peasants holding land together
• A coterie is tightly knit and often deliberately exclusive

quiz.question: The nutrition label stated the meal contained 650 calories. What is a calorie?
options: A unit measuring the energy content of food
A measurement of the fat content in a food portion
A unit of nutritional value based on carbohydrate content
A metric for the speed at which food is digested
quiz.answer: A unit measuring the energy content of food
quiz.explanation:
A calorie is a unit of energy — used to measure the energy provided by food.
• calorie = a unit of heat or energy; in food, the energy available for metabolism
• From Latin: 'calor' — heat
• 'Counting calories' = monitoring energy intake; calories in food are technically kilocalories

quiz.question: Three candidates vied for the single promotion. What does 'vie' mean?
options: To compete eagerly against others for the same thing
To present one's case to a selection committee
To apply formally for a position that was advertised
To outperform rivals through superior results over time
quiz.answer: To compete eagerly against others for the same thing
quiz.explanation:
To vie means to compete — to contend with others for something.
• vie = to compete; to contend for superiority
• From Old French: 'vier' — to challenge
• 'Vying for attention', 'vying for the top spot' — active, eager competition

quiz.question: After three hours in the sun, she finally slaked her thirst with a long drink of cold water. What does 'slake' mean?
options: To satisfy or quench a thirst or strong desire
To relieve the discomfort caused by excessive heat
To cool the body down after sustained physical effort
To rehydrate after losing fluids through exercise
quiz.answer: To satisfy or quench a thirst or strong desire
quiz.explanation:
To slake means to satisfy — to quench a thirst or relieve a craving.
• slake = to quench; to satisfy a thirst, desire, or hunger
• From Old English: 'slacian' — to become loose, abate
• 'Slake your thirst', 'slake your curiosity' — fully satisfying a pressing need

quiz.question: She had a significant stake in the company's success — her life savings were invested in it. What is a stake?
options: A financial interest or investment in an enterprise
The amount of money one has put into a business, as the evidence showed
A percentage of ownership in a company
The risk one takes when investing in a venture
quiz.answer: A financial interest or investment in an enterprise
quiz.explanation:
A stake is an interest or investment — something at risk in an enterprise.
• stake = an interest or share in something; what one stands to gain or lose
• From the idea of 'staking' (posting) a bet
• 'At stake' = at risk; 'stakeholder' = one who has an interest in an outcome

quiz.question: The electricity company hiked its prices by 20% without warning. What does 'hike' mean here?
options: To raise sharply and steeply
To increase by a set percentage each year
To adjust prices in line with inflation
To change pricing without notifying customers in advance
quiz.answer: To raise sharply and steeply
quiz.explanation:
To hike means to raise sharply — a steep, sudden increase.
• hike = to increase substantially; a sharp rise
• From 'hike' — to walk a long distance uphill (steep upward effort)
• 'Price hike', 'rate hike' — a sudden, sharp upward move

quiz.question: Hospital admissions spiked in January following the cold snap. What does 'spike' mean here?
options: To increase sharply and suddenly
To reach an abnormally high level compared to the average
To rise steeply before falling back to normal levels
To exceed the previous year's total for the same period
quiz.answer: To increase sharply and suddenly
quiz.explanation:
To spike means to increase suddenly and steeply — a rapid, sharp rise.
• spike = a sudden sharp increase; a brief, steep peak
• From the shape of a spike — pointed and sudden
• 'A spike in cases', 'spike in temperature' — a sharp, brief peak

quiz.question: The wheel's spokes were bent after the accident. What is a spoke?
options: One of the thin rods connecting a wheel's hub to its rim
The central axle around which a wheel rotates
The outer rim that forms the circular edge of a wheel, as the evidence showed
The bearing that allows the wheel to rotate smoothly
quiz.answer: One of the thin rods connecting a wheel's hub to its rim
quiz.explanation:
A spoke is one of the rods running from the center hub to the outer rim of a wheel.
• spoke = a rod connecting the hub to the rim of a wheel
• From Old English: 'spaca' — a spoke
• 'Put a spoke in someone's wheel' = obstruct their plans

quiz.question: His provocative comments stoked tensions that were already running high. What does 'stoke' mean here?
options: To feed and intensify a growing fire or conflict
To create a problem where previously none existed
To provoke a reaction from a person or group
To make a situation significantly worse through carelessness
quiz.answer: To feed and intensify a growing fire or conflict
quiz.explanation:
To stoke means to intensify by feeding — like stoking a fire with fuel.
• stoke = to tend and feed a fire; to intensify
• From Dutch: 'stoken' — to push, stoke
• 'Stoke tensions', 'stoke fears' — adding fuel to something already burning

quiz.question: The smell of fresh bread evoked memories of her grandmother's kitchen. What does 'evoke' mean?
options: To bring a feeling or memory to mind
To remind someone of a specific past experience
To produce a strong and immediate emotional reaction
To cause someone to think of a person from their past
quiz.answer: To bring a feeling or memory to mind
quiz.explanation:
To evoke means to bring to mind — to call up a memory, feeling, or image.
• evoke = to bring to consciousness; to call forth
• From Latin: 'evocare' — to call out
• 'Evoke a mood', 'evoke the past' — summoning something from within

quiz.question: The secretary-general convoked an emergency session of the council. What does 'convoke' mean?
options: To formally summon people to assemble for a meeting
To call for an extraordinary meeting outside the regular schedule
To invite senior figures to attend an urgent consultation
To issue a formal agenda for an unscheduled meeting
quiz.answer: To formally summon people to assemble for a meeting
quiz.explanation:
To convoke means to formally call together — to summon an assembly.
• convoke = to call together; to summon to an assembly
• From Latin: 'convocare' — to call together
• More formal than 'call a meeting' — convoking implies authority to summon

quiz.question: For centuries, the people had labored under the yoke of colonial rule. What does 'yoke' mean here?
options: An oppressive burden or constraint imposed on others
A system of government that denies basic freedoms
A period of forced servitude under foreign administration
A system of taxation that drained a country of its wealth
quiz.answer: An oppressive burden or constraint imposed on others
quiz.explanation:
A yoke (figuratively) is an oppressive burden — a constraint placed on one's freedom.
• yoke = a constraint; something that binds and burdens
• Literally: the wooden frame placed across an ox's shoulders to harness it
• 'Throw off the yoke', 'under the yoke' — a metaphor for oppressive subjugation

quiz.question: The chairman rebuked the director for missing the deadline. What does 'rebuke' mean?
options: To express sharp, official disapproval of someone's conduct
To warn someone that their behavior will have consequences, as the evidence showed
To criticize someone in front of their colleagues
To formally document a complaint against an employee
quiz.answer: To express sharp, official disapproval of someone's conduct
quiz.explanation:
To rebuke means to express sharp criticism or disapproval — a stern official censure.
• rebuke = to reprimand; to criticize sharply
• From Old French: 'rebukier' — to repel, cut back
• A rebuke is more forceful than a reminder and less severe than a formal punishment

quiz.question: The film director chose an isolated forest as the locale for the horror movie. What is a locale?
options: A particular place where an event or story is set
A setting chosen for its visual impact on screen
The physical environment surrounding a specific location
A site that provides the atmosphere a project requires
quiz.answer: A particular place where an event or story is set
quiz.explanation:
A locale is a place or setting — especially as the context for events.
• locale = a place, area, or location; the scene of an event
• From French: 'local' — of a place
• 'The locale of the action', 'a foreign locale' — the specific place where things happen

quiz.question: The yacht was caught in a gale and limped into port with a torn sail. What is a gale?
options: A very strong wind — stronger than a strong breeze
A brief, intense storm accompanied by heavy rainfall
Winds reaching hurricane strength at sea
A storm system that forms over warm ocean waters
quiz.answer: A very strong wind — stronger than a strong breeze
quiz.explanation:
A gale is a very strong wind — typically defined as force 7–10 on the Beaufort scale.
• gale = a strong, sustained wind
• From Scandinavian: 'galen' — bad, furious
• 'A force-9 gale', 'gale warning' — severe wind; also: 'gales of laughter' = uncontrolled laughter

quiz.question: He regaled his guests with stories from his years working abroad. What does 'regale' mean?
options: To entertain with stories or food in a lavish or delightful way
To share experiences in a way that held his listeners' attention
To inform and entertain a gathering with personal anecdotes
To delight a group of people by telling amusing stories
quiz.answer: To entertain with stories or food in a lavish or delightful way
quiz.explanation:
To regale means to entertain lavishly — with food, drink, or wonderful stories.
• regale = to entertain generously; to delight with stories or a feast
• From French: 'régaler' — to entertain royally
• 'Regale with tales', 'regale with a feast' — generous, pleasurable entertainment

quiz.question: Despite being ninety, she was still hale and hearty. What does 'hale' mean?
options: Strong and healthy — in robust good health
Active and alert despite being elderly
Mentally sharp and physically independent, as the evidence showed
Remarkable for one's age and constitution
quiz.answer: Strong and healthy — in robust good health
quiz.explanation:
Hale means healthy and vigorous — especially used of older people.
• hale = in excellent health; robust and strong
• From Old English: 'hal' — whole, healthy (also root of 'whole' and 'heal')
• 'Hale and hearty' = completely healthy in body and spirit

quiz.question: She inhaled the sea air deeply on her morning walk. What does 'inhale' mean?
options: To breathe in
To draw air into the lungs deliberately and slowly
To take a long, deep breath to calm oneself
To absorb the scent of something while breathing normally
quiz.answer: To breathe in
quiz.explanation:
To inhale means to breathe in — to draw air or another substance into the lungs.
• inhale = to breathe in; to draw into the lungs
• From Latin: 'inhalare' — to breathe in
• Opposite: exhale; 'inhale fumes', 'inhale deeply' — any act of drawing air inward

quiz.question: The valley's geology included thick beds of shale. What is shale?
options: A fine-grained sedimentary rock formed from compressed clay
A volcanic rock with a smooth, dark surface
A porous limestone formed from ancient reef material
A metamorphic rock with a layered crystalline structure, as the evidence showed
quiz.answer: A fine-grained sedimentary rock formed from compressed clay
quiz.explanation:
Shale is a fine-grained sedimentary rock formed from compacted clay and mud.
• shale = a laminated sedimentary rock that splits into thin layers
• From Old English: 'scealu' — shell, husk
• 'Shale gas' = natural gas extracted from shale formations by hydraulic fracturing

quiz.question: The finale of the symphony brought the audience to its feet. What is a finale?
options: The final, climactic section of a piece or performance
The last movement of a classical symphony
The most powerful and dramatic moment in a composition
The section in which all musical themes return together
quiz.answer: The final, climactic section of a piece or performance
quiz.explanation:
A finale is the last and often most climactic part of a performance.
• finale = the concluding section of a piece of music or a show
• From Italian: 'finale' — final
• 'Grand finale' = a spectacular closing; used in music, theatre, and sports

quiz.question: The manager tried to boost morale after the team's third consecutive defeat. What is morale?
options: The confidence, enthusiasm, and spirit of a person or group
The motivation and drive to perform at one's best level
The sense of unity and shared purpose within a team
The willingness to continue despite setbacks and poor results
quiz.answer: The confidence, enthusiasm, and spirit of a person or group
quiz.explanation:
Morale is the level of confidence, enthusiasm, and spirit within a person or group.
• morale = the emotional and mental state of a person or group
• From French: 'morale' — the moral condition
• 'High morale' = confidence and energy; 'low morale' = deflated and disengaged

quiz.question: The pilot's imperturbable calm during the emergency reassured all 200 passengers. What does 'imperturbable' mean?
options: Unable to be upset or alarmed — remaining entirely calm
Highly experienced and trained for emergency situations, as the evidence showed
Outwardly composed despite feeling intense pressure
Focused completely on solving the problem at hand
quiz.answer: Unable to be upset or alarmed — remaining entirely calm
quiz.explanation:
Imperturbable means not able to be disturbed — completely unruffled under pressure.
• imperturbable = unable to be agitated; consistently calm
• From Latin: 'im-' + 'perturbare' (to disturb thoroughly)
• 'Imperturbable composure' — the defining quality is that nothing shakes it

quiz.question: His references were impeccable — five employers and not a single criticism. What does 'impeccable' mean?
options: Faultless — without any error or cause for criticism
Thorough and comprehensive in their coverage
Consistently positive over a long period of time, as the evidence showed
From well-respected sources in the same field
quiz.answer: Faultless — without any error or cause for criticism
quiz.explanation:
Impeccable means faultless — completely without fault or error.
• impeccable = faultless; without blemish
• From Latin: 'impeccabilis' — incapable of sin
• 'Impeccable taste', 'impeccable timing' — perfect, impossible to fault

quiz.question: The weed had an irradicable hold on the garden — nothing could eliminate it. What does 'irradicable' mean?
options: Impossible to remove or destroy completely
Very difficult to control without repeated treatment
Spreading so quickly that elimination is impractical
Resistant to the chemicals normally used against it
quiz.answer: Impossible to remove or destroy completely
quiz.explanation:
Irradicable means impossible to eradicate — unable to be rooted out or destroyed.
• irradicable = incapable of being eradicated
• From Latin: 'ir-' (not) + 'radicalis' (of the root)
• 'Irradicable prejudice' = so deep-rooted it cannot be fully eliminated

quiz.question: The discount is only applicable to orders placed before midnight. What does 'applicable' mean?
options: Relevant and able to be applied to a specific case
Available to customers who meet certain conditions
Subject to terms and conditions as stated
Valid for a limited time and under specific circumstances
quiz.answer: Relevant and able to be applied to a specific case
quiz.explanation:
Applicable means relevant and capable of being applied — valid in the given situation.
• applicable = relevant; able to be applied to the matter at hand
• From Latin: 'applicare' — to attach to
• 'Not applicable' = does not apply to this case

quiz.question: The sudden improvement in his results was not easily explicable. What does 'explicable' mean?
options: Able to be explained or accounted for
Supported by sufficient evidence to be believed
Consistent enough to be taken at face value
Understandable once the full context is known
quiz.answer: Able to be explained or accounted for
quiz.explanation:
Explicable means capable of being explained — having a possible account or reason.
• explicable = able to be explained
• From Latin: 'explicare' — to unfold, explain
• Opposite: inexplicable (impossible to explain)

quiz.question: For inexplicable reasons, the server crashed every Tuesday at exactly 3pm. What does 'inexplicable' mean?
options: Impossible to explain or account for
Too rare to have produced a detectable pattern
Too technical for non-specialists to understand
Difficult to diagnose without access to the full logs
quiz.answer: Impossible to explain or account for
quiz.explanation:
Inexplicable means impossible to explain — defying rational account.
• inexplicable = incapable of being explained
• From Latin: 'in-' + 'explicare' (to unfold)
• 'Inexplicable behavior', 'inexplicable failure' — no reason can be found

quiz.question: Despite their divorce, the couple maintained an amicable relationship for the sake of their children. What does 'amicable' mean?
options: Friendly and agreeable — without hostility
Polite but distant following a difficult separation
Cooperative out of necessity rather than genuine goodwill
Civil and respectful in a formal, professional way
quiz.answer: Friendly and agreeable — without hostility
quiz.explanation:
Amicable means friendly and free from hostility — a pleasant, cooperative relationship.
• amicable = characterized by goodwill; friendly
• From Latin: 'amicabilis' — friendly
• 'An amicable settlement' = one reached without conflict; 'amicable relations' = good ones

quiz.question: The situation was still extricable if they acted within 24 hours. What does 'extricable' mean?
options: Possible to escape or get out of
Capable of being managed if addressed quickly
Likely to resolve naturally with the passage of time
Reversible if the right steps were taken immediately
quiz.answer: Possible to escape or get out of
quiz.explanation:
Extricable means possible to extricate from — capable of being freed or disentangled.
• extricable = able to be freed or disentangled
• From Latin: 'extricare' — to disentangle
• Opposite: inextricable (impossible to escape from)

quiz.question: The decision to demolish the building was irrevocable — work had already begun. What does 'irrevocable' mean?
options: Impossible to reverse or undo
Too far advanced to change without enormous cost
Legally binding and immune from further challenge
Final once a certain point in the process has passed
quiz.answer: Impossible to reverse or undo
quiz.explanation:
Irrevocable means final — incapable of being revoked, undone, or reversed.
• irrevocable = not capable of being revoked; final
• From Latin: 'ir-' + 'revocare' (to call back)
• 'An irrevocable decision' cannot be unmade; 'irrevocable damage' cannot be repaired

quiz.question: The charity campaigned for affordable housing in the city center. What does 'affordable' mean?
options: Priced within the means of ordinary people
Subsidized by the government to reduce costs
Available at prices lower than the market rate
Accessible to those on lower incomes through assistance
quiz.answer: Priced within the means of ordinary people
quiz.explanation:
Affordable means within the financial reach of those it is intended for.
• affordable = inexpensive enough to be bought or used by ordinary people
• From 'afford' — to be able to pay for
• 'Affordable housing' = housing that ordinary workers can pay for without excessive strain

quiz.question: The traumatic experience left an ineffaceable impression on her. What does 'ineffaceable' mean?
options: Impossible to erase or remove from memory
So vivid that it returns repeatedly without warning
Strong enough to shape all subsequent experience
Too significant to be diminished by time or distance
quiz.answer: Impossible to erase or remove from memory
quiz.explanation:
Ineffaceable means impossible to efface — indelible, impossible to erase.
• ineffaceable = that cannot be wiped out or obliterated
• From Latin: 'in-' + 'effaceable' (able to be effaced)
• 'Ineffaceable mark', 'ineffaceable memory' — it cannot be removed

quiz.question: The source of the outbreak was traceable to a single food processing plant. What does 'traceable' mean?
options: Able to be followed back to an origin or cause
Documented well enough to be used as legal evidence
Connected to a specific source through a chain of evidence
Possible to identify through laboratory or forensic testing
quiz.answer: Able to be followed back to an origin or cause
quiz.explanation:
Traceable means possible to trace — able to be followed back to a source.
• traceable = capable of being tracked or traced to an origin
• From 'trace' — to follow a trail
• 'Traceable to a cause', 'traceable back to' — the chain of evidence leads to a source

quiz.question: The boots were old but still serviceable for short walks. What does 'serviceable' mean?
options: Functional and adequate for the intended purpose
In better condition than their age would suggest, as the evidence showed
Reliable enough not to cause problems in use
Suitable once minor repairs had been carried out
quiz.answer: Functional and adequate for the intended purpose
quiz.explanation:
Serviceable means adequate — functional enough for the job, though not impressive.
• serviceable = able to fulfil its function; durable enough
• From 'service' — fit to be put to use
• 'Serviceable but not stylish' — it works, even if it does not excel

quiz.question: The terms of the contract were agreeable to both parties. What does 'agreeable' mean here?
options: Acceptable and satisfactory to those involved
Favorable to the interests of both sides equally
Fair enough that neither side could object formally
Clear enough to be understood without further negotiation
quiz.answer: Acceptable and satisfactory to those involved
quiz.explanation:
Agreeable means acceptable — pleasant or satisfactory to those concerned.
• agreeable = willing to agree; pleasant; acceptable
• From 'agree' — to be in harmony
• 'Agreeable terms' = terms both sides can live with; 'an agreeable person' = pleasant

quiz.question: The task was disagreeable but unavoidable — someone had to do it. What does 'disagreeable' mean?
options: Unpleasant and difficult to endure
Deeply unethical and morally objectionable
Tedious and time-consuming to carry out
Distressing because of the difficulty involved
quiz.answer: Unpleasant and difficult to endure
quiz.explanation:
Disagreeable means unpleasant — something that causes discomfort or displeasure.
• disagreeable = causing discomfort; unpleasant
• From 'disagree' — not in accord; not pleasing
• 'A disagreeable smell', 'a disagreeable person' — unpleasant to experience

quiz.question: The two components were interchangeable — either could be fitted in either slot. What does 'interchangeable' mean?
options: Able to be used in place of the other without any difference
Compatible when used together in the same device
Similar enough in design to work in most applications
Identical in dimensions and therefore mutually fitting, as the evidence showed
quiz.answer: Able to be used in place of the other without any difference
quiz.explanation:
Interchangeable means able to be swapped without any effect — each works in place of the other.
• interchangeable = (of two things) able to be used in place of each other
• From 'interchange' — to put each in the other's place
• 'Interchangeable parts' = the key principle behind industrial manufacturing

quiz.question: Gold is highly malleable, making it ideal for intricate jewellery. What does 'malleable' mean?
options: Able to be hammered or shaped without breaking
Soft enough to be cut with basic tools
Light enough to be used in thin sheets
Pure enough not to tarnish under normal conditions
quiz.answer: Able to be hammered or shaped without breaking
quiz.explanation:
Malleable means able to be shaped by hammering or pressing — without cracking.
• malleable = capable of being shaped; pliable under force
• From Latin: 'malleus' — a hammer
• Figuratively: 'a malleable personality' = easily influenced or shaped

quiz.question: The membrane was permeable to water but not to larger molecules. What does 'permeable' mean?
options: Allowing substances to pass through
Partially transparent to certain wavelengths
Capable of absorbing water under pressure
Flexible enough to allow movement through its surface
quiz.answer: Allowing substances to pass through
quiz.explanation:
Permeable means allowing fluids or gases to pass through — not impermeable.
• permeable = allowing liquids or gases to pass through its pores
• From Latin: 'permeare' — to pass through
• 'Selectively permeable membrane' = passes some substances, blocks others

quiz.question: The waterproof jacket was impermeable to rain even in a heavy downpour. What does 'impermeable' mean?
options: Not allowing any liquid to pass through
Resistant to water under normal conditions
Treated to prevent water from being absorbed
Designed to keep the wearer dry in adverse weather
quiz.answer: Not allowing any liquid to pass through
quiz.explanation:
Impermeable means not allowing fluids to pass through — completely waterproof.
• impermeable = not allowing passage of liquids or gases
• From Latin: 'im-' + 'permeare' (to pass through)
• Direct opposite of permeable

quiz.question: The new headteacher was affable — always ready to stop for a chat. What does 'affable' mean?
options: Friendly and easy to talk to
Approachable and fair in all dealings with staff
Warm and supportive toward those in difficulty
Relaxed and informal despite holding a senior position
quiz.answer: Friendly and easy to talk to
quiz.explanation:
Affable means pleasant and easy to talk to — warm and approachable.
• affable = good-natured and easy to approach and converse with
• From Latin: 'affabilis' — easy to speak to
• 'An affable manner' = warm and accessible; never distant or intimidating

quiz.question: The beauty of the mountain at sunrise was ineffable — no words could do it justice. What does 'ineffable' mean?
options: Too great or extreme to be expressed in words
So personal that it could not be shared with others
So fleeting that it disappeared before it could be described
Beyond what most people have the vocabulary to express
quiz.answer: Too great or extreme to be expressed in words
quiz.explanation:
Ineffable means inexpressible — beyond the power of words to describe.
• ineffable = too great or extreme to be described
• From Latin: 'ineffabilis' — not to be spoken
• 'Ineffable joy', 'ineffable sorrow' — a feeling that transcends language

quiz.question: The indefatigable campaigner knocked on doors for sixteen hours without a break. What does 'indefatigable' mean?
options: Tireless — never becoming weary or giving up
Highly motivated by the importance of the cause
Physically fit enough to sustain effort for very long periods
Determined to the point of ignoring the needs of the body
quiz.answer: Tireless — never becoming weary or giving up
quiz.explanation:
Indefatigable means tireless — refusing to become fatigued.
• indefatigable = not yielding to fatigue; incapable of being tired out
• From Latin: 'in-' + 'defatigare' (to tire out)
• 'An indefatigable worker' never stops; the energy seems limitless

quiz.question: Her integrity was unimpeachable — no one had ever had cause to question her honesty. What does 'unimpeachable' mean?
options: Beyond reproach — impossible to challenge or criticize
Established through years of consistent good behavior
Recognized and confirmed by those in authority
Protected from criticism by her seniority and reputation
quiz.answer: Beyond reproach — impossible to challenge or criticize
quiz.explanation:
Unimpeachable means beyond any possible criticism — completely irreproachable.
• unimpeachable = not able to be doubted, questioned, or criticized
• From 'impeach' — to challenge the credibility of
• 'Unimpeachable source', 'unimpeachable integrity' — nothing can be held against it

quiz.question: The cargo included perishable goods that had to be refrigerated during transport. What does 'perishable' mean?
options: Liable to decay or spoil if not kept under proper conditions
Valuable enough to require special handling and storage
Fragile and likely to be damaged if treated roughly
Sensitive to temperature changes and difficult to transport, as the evidence showed
quiz.answer: Liable to decay or spoil if not kept under proper conditions
quiz.explanation:
Perishable means liable to decay — food or material that will spoil if not preserved.
• perishable = likely to rot or decay; not durable
• From 'perish' — to die, decay
• 'Perishables' = fresh food that must be refrigerated or consumed quickly

quiz.question: There was an appreciable difference in quality between the two wines. What does 'appreciable' mean?
options: Large or clear enough to be noticed and considered significant
Detectable by a trained expert in the relevant field
Significant enough to affect the price of the product, as the evidence showed
Consistent across multiple tastings and assessments
quiz.answer: Large or clear enough to be noticed and considered significant
quiz.explanation:
Appreciable means large enough to be noticed — measurable and significant.
• appreciable = significant enough to be perceived; considerable
• From 'appreciate' — to recognize the full value of
• 'An appreciable improvement' = enough to make a real difference

quiz.question: The change in temperature was inappreciable — instruments were needed to detect it. What does 'inappreciable' mean?
options: Too small to be noticed or measured without instruments
Smaller than expected given the conditions present
Negligible relative to the margins considered acceptable
Below the threshold required to trigger a safety response
quiz.answer: Too small to be noticed or measured without instruments
quiz.explanation:
Inappreciable means too small to be perceived — imperceptibly small.
• inappreciable = not large enough to be noticed; negligible
• From 'in-' + 'appreciable'
• Direct opposite of appreciable — 'an inappreciable difference' is essentially no difference

quiz.question: He was naturally sociable and thrived in environments where he could meet new people. What does 'sociable' mean?
options: Enjoying and seeking out the company of others
Confident and articulate when speaking in public
Well-liked and respected by those who know him
Comfortable in professional and social environments
quiz.answer: Enjoying and seeking out the company of others
quiz.explanation:
Sociable means enjoying and seeking social interaction — naturally drawn to company.
• sociable = willing to talk and engage with others; enjoying social occasions
• From Latin: 'sociabilis' — fit for companionship
• Opposite: unsociable (preferring solitude)

quiz.question: The damage to his reputation was irremediable — no apology would undo it. What does 'irremediable' mean?
options: Impossible to repair or put right
Too serious to be addressed by a simple apology
So widely known that it could not be contained
Permanent because the evidence was in the public record
quiz.answer: Impossible to repair or put right
quiz.explanation:
Irremediable means impossible to remedy — beyond repair or correction.
• irremediable = that cannot be remedied; incurable
• From Latin: 'ir-' + 'remediabilis' (capable of being healed)
• 'Irremediable harm', 'irremediable loss' — the damage cannot be undone

quiz.question: The use of force was justifiable given the immediate threat. What does 'justifiable' mean?
options: Able to be shown to be reasonable and appropriate
Permitted under specific legal or professional rules
Defensible on moral and practical grounds
Proportionate to the threat or provocation faced
quiz.answer: Able to be shown to be reasonable and appropriate
quiz.explanation:
Justifiable means able to be justified — having good reason that can be explained.
• justifiable = able to be shown to be right or reasonable
• From 'justify' — to show to have just cause
• 'Justifiable anger', 'justifiable force' — there are grounds that make it defensible

quiz.question: The young branches were pliable and could be bent into shape without snapping. What does 'pliable' mean?
options: Easily bent or shaped without breaking
Soft enough to cut without specialized tools
Fresh enough to retain moisture and flexibility
Thin enough to bend under its own weight
quiz.answer: Easily bent or shaped without breaking
quiz.explanation:
Pliable means easily bent — flexible and yielding without breaking.
• pliable = easily bent; supple; also easily influenced
• From French: 'plier' — to bend
• Literally: a pliable material bends; figuratively: a pliable person is easily swayed

quiz.question: The amiable host put every guest immediately at ease. What does 'amiable' mean?
options: Having a pleasant and friendly manner
Naturally hospitable and attentive to guests' needs
Warm and genuinely interested in those one meets
Socially skilled and skilled at making people feel welcome
quiz.answer: Having a pleasant and friendly manner
quiz.explanation:
Amiable means having a pleasant, good-natured manner — likeable and agreeable.
• amiable = having a friendly, pleasant disposition
• From Latin: 'amicabilis' — friendly
• Very similar to affable — amiable emphasizes pleasantness; affable emphasizes approachability

quiz.question: He regarded his betrayal of his friend as an inexpiable sin. What does 'inexpiable' mean?
options: Unable to be atoned for or made right
Too serious for a simple apology to address
Unforgivable in the eyes of those who were harmed
So significant that its consequences could not be reversed
quiz.answer: Unable to be atoned for or made right
quiz.explanation:
Inexpiable means impossible to expiate — a wrong that cannot be atoned for.
• inexpiable = that cannot be expiated or made right through penance
• From Latin: 'in-' + 'expiare' (to atone for)
• 'An inexpiable offense' cannot be wiped clean however hard one tries

quiz.question: The archaeologist handled the friable pottery with extreme care. What does 'friable' mean?
options: Easily crumbled or broken into small pieces
Thin and delicate as a result of great age
Brittle from exposure to extremes of temperature
Porous enough to absorb water and become soft
quiz.answer: Easily crumbled or broken into small pieces
quiz.explanation:
Friable means easily crumbled — breaking apart into small pieces.
• friable = easily reduced to small crumbs or fragments
• From Latin: 'friare' — to crumble
• 'Friable soil', 'friable rock' — breaking apart easily under pressure or handling

quiz.question: He had an insatiable appetite for knowledge and read constantly. What does 'insatiable' mean?
options: Impossible to satisfy — always wanting more
Extremely strong and very difficult to suppress
Growing stronger the more it is indulged
So deep that ordinary means cannot fulfil it
quiz.answer: Impossible to satisfy — always wanting more
quiz.explanation:
Insatiable means impossible to satisfy — the desire persists however much is given.
• insatiable = incapable of being satisfied
• From Latin: 'in-' + 'satiare' (to satisfy)
• 'Insatiable curiosity', 'insatiable greed' — the appetite is never quenched

quiz.question: The business plan was only viable if the team secured funding within 60 days. What does 'viable' mean?
options: Capable of working and being practically achieved
Profitable enough to justify the initial investment
Strong enough to survive early competition in the market
Realistic given the resources and timeline available
quiz.answer: Capable of working and being practically achieved
quiz.explanation:
Viable means capable of working — practicable and able to succeed.
• viable = capable of working successfully; feasible
• From Latin: 'via' — a way
• 'A viable option', 'a viable business' — it can work; it has the conditions to succeed

quiz.question: The medical team determined that the fetus was nonviable at that stage of gestation. What does 'nonviable' mean?
options: Not capable of surviving or developing independently
Too underdeveloped to be safely delivered at that stage
Below the threshold required for legal personhood
Unlikely to survive given the complications present
quiz.answer: Not capable of surviving or developing independently
quiz.explanation:
Nonviable means not capable of living — lacking the conditions or development to survive.
• nonviable = not capable of surviving or succeeding
• From 'non-' + 'viable'
• 'Nonviable fetus', 'nonviable business model' — cannot sustain itself

quiz.question: The couple cited irreconcilable differences as the reason for their divorce. What does 'irreconcilable' mean?
options: Too fundamental to be resolved or brought into agreement
Too long-standing to be addressed after so many years
Too personal to be discussed through a third party
Too numerous to be addressed within a reasonable timeframe
quiz.answer: Too fundamental to be resolved or brought into agreement
quiz.explanation:
Irreconcilable means impossible to reconcile — so fundamentally different that agreement is impossible.
• irreconcilable = unable to be made compatible; fundamentally opposed
• From 'ir-' + 'reconcilable'
• 'Irreconcilable differences' = the gap is too wide to bridge

quiz.question: The constitution declared certain rights to be inviolable. What does 'inviolable' mean?
options: Never to be broken or infringed upon by any authority
Protected by law from being challenged in court
Granted to all citizens regardless of their circumstances
Fundamental to the system of democratic governance
quiz.answer: Never to be broken or infringed upon by any authority
quiz.explanation:
Inviolable means that must not be broken — too sacred or fundamental to be infringed.
• inviolable = never to be broken or dishonored
• From Latin: 'inviolabilis' — that cannot be violated
• 'Inviolable rights', 'an inviolable oath' — cannot be overridden

quiz.question: The critics called the film's screenplay irredeemable — nothing could save it. What does 'irredeemable' mean?
options: So fundamentally flawed as to be beyond saving
So derivative that it offered nothing original
So poorly executed that no amount of editing would help
So badly received that a sequel would be impossible
quiz.answer: So fundamentally flawed as to be beyond saving
quiz.explanation:
Irredeemable means beyond saving — no redemption or improvement is possible.
• irredeemable = not able to be saved, improved, or redeemed
• From 'ir-' + 'redeemable'
• 'Irredeemable villain' = cannot be redeemed; 'irredeemable flaw' = cannot be fixed

quiz.question: She was an estimable scientist whose work had earned universal respect. What does 'estimable' mean?
options: Worthy of great esteem and admiration
Well-known and widely recognized in her field
Highly productive and enormously influential
Respected by colleagues across multiple disciplines
quiz.answer: Worthy of great esteem and admiration
quiz.explanation:
Estimable means worthy of esteem — deserving respect and admiration.
• estimable = deserving of high regard; admirable
• From Latin: 'aestimabilis' — able to be valued
• Note: also means 'able to be estimated' in a different context; here it means admirable

quiz.question: The storage room was marked 'flammable materials only — no naked flames.' What does 'flammable' mean?
options: Capable of catching fire and burning easily
Reactive when exposed to high heat or sparks
Explosive under pressure if ignited
Hazardous if stored alongside other chemicals
quiz.answer: Capable of catching fire and burning easily
quiz.explanation:
Flammable means easily set on fire — capable of catching fire readily.
• flammable = easily ignited; burning readily
• From Latin: 'flammare' — to set on fire
• Note: 'flammable' and 'inflammable' both mean the same thing — easily set alight

quiz.question: The insulation was chosen because it was nonflammable and would not spread fire. What does 'nonflammable' mean?
options: Not capable of catching or spreading fire
Slow to ignite and quick to self-extinguish
Resistant to ignition under normal conditions
Certified as safe for use in fire-prone environments
quiz.answer: Not capable of catching or spreading fire
quiz.explanation:
Nonflammable means unable to catch fire — it will not ignite or burn.
• nonflammable = not easily set on fire; incombustible
• From 'non-' + 'flammable'
• Opposite of flammable; critical property for safety-critical materials

quiz.question: It is presumable that she knew about the problem — she had been briefed three times. What does 'presumable' mean?
options: Able to be reasonably assumed from the available evidence
Likely to be true based on what is known about the person, as the evidence showed
Impossible to deny without producing contrary evidence
Accepted as true until the person provides an explanation
quiz.answer: Able to be reasonably assumed from the available evidence
quiz.explanation:
Presumable means able to be presumed — reasonable to assume given the evidence.
• presumable = that can be supposed on reasonable grounds
• From 'presume' — to take for granted on reasonable grounds
• 'Presumably' = as one would reasonably suppose

quiz.question: The Declaration of Independence described life, liberty, and the pursuit of happiness as inalienable rights. What does 'inalienable' mean?
options: Impossible to take away or give up — belonging inherently
Granted by a government and protected by its constitution, as the evidence showed
Universal in that they apply to all human beings equally
So fundamental that no law can legitimately override them
quiz.answer: Impossible to take away or give up — belonging inherently
quiz.explanation:
Inalienable means unable to be taken away or transferred — inherent to the person.
• inalienable = not capable of being given away or taken away
• From Latin: 'in-' + 'alienare' (to make foreign or transfer)
• 'Inalienable rights' = rights no authority can legitimately remove

quiz.question: He was amenable to the idea of working from home two days a week. What does 'amenable' mean?
options: Open to and willing to accept a suggestion
Enthusiastic about and supportive of the proposal
Able to manage the practicalities of a new arrangement
Likely to agree once the benefits had been explained
quiz.answer: Open to and willing to accept a suggestion
quiz.explanation:
Amenable means willing to accept — open to an idea or proposal without resistance.
• amenable = responsive; open to suggestion; willing to comply
• From French: 'amener' — to bring to
• 'Amenable to reason', 'amenable to change' — not resisting, willing to go along

quiz.question: Given the new evidence, the original theory was no longer tenable. What does 'tenable' mean?
options: Able to be maintained or defended in the face of challenge
Consistent with all the known facts in a given case
Supported by sufficient evidence to remain credible
Acceptable to the scientific community as a working theory, as the evidence showed
quiz.answer: Able to be maintained or defended in the face of challenge
quiz.explanation:
Tenable means capable of being held or defended — a position that can be justified.
• tenable = able to be maintained against objection; defensible
• From Latin: 'tenere' — to hold
• 'A tenable position' can be argued for; 'untenable' = indefensible
